questions.question: An office is BEST described as

options.A: A room where only the managing director works
options.B: A place where clerical and administrative work is carried out to support the operations of an organisation
options.C: A building where goods are manufactured and sold
options.D: A room used exclusively for storing files and documents

questions.explanation: An office is the administrative nerve centre where clerical, administrative, and managerial activities are carried out — including communication, record-keeping, and coordination.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary function of an office?

options.A: Manufacturing goods for sale
options.B: Receiving and sending information, keeping records, and coordinating business activities
options.C: Recruiting and training factory workers
options.D: Distributing goods to retail outlets

questions.explanation: Primary office functions include receiving and dispatching information, recording data, processing transactions, communicating, and coordinating organisational activities.

questions.question: An open-plan office is one in which

options.A: The office is open to the public at all times
options.B: All staff work in one large undivided space without permanent internal walls
options.C: Staff can choose their own working hours
options.D: The office is used for both manufacturing and administration

questions.explanation: An open-plan office is a large undivided work area where staff work together without permanent partitions, improving communication and supervision but reducing privacy.

questions.question: A private (cellular) office arrangement is one in which

options.A: All staff share one large open workspace
options.B: Individual employees or small teams occupy separate, enclosed rooms
options.C: The office is restricted to senior management only
options.D: Staff work from home using private computers

questions.explanation: A private (cellular) office has individual rooms separated by permanent walls, providing privacy and reducing noise — suitable for confidential work.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of an open-plan office?

options.A: Greater privacy for each employee
options.B: Better supervision and communication among staff
options.C: Reduced noise levels in the workplace
options.D: More confidentiality when handling sensitive matters

questions.explanation: Open-plan offices improve communication, allow better supervision, make better use of space, and are cheaper to build and maintain than private offices.

questions.question: The main purpose of an office is to

options.A: Generate revenue by selling products to customers
options.B: Serve as the administrative nerve centre — collecting, processing, storing, and disseminating information to support the organisation
options.C: Manufacture goods for distribution to retailers
options.D: Provide recreational facilities for employees

questions.explanation: The office is the administrative hub of the organisation — handling information and coordinating activities that support the entire organisation's operations.

questions.question: A back office differs from a front office in that the back office

options.A: Is located at the back of the building
options.B: Handles administrative, processing, and support functions with no direct contact with external customers
options.C: Deals directly with customers and clients on the front line
options.D: Is used exclusively by senior management

questions.explanation: The back office handles administrative and support functions (accounting, payroll, IT) invisible to customers. The front office (reception, sales) deals directly with clients.

questions.question: An office manager is responsible for

options.A: Only typing letters and filing documents
options.B: Planning, organising, directing, and controlling all office activities and staff
options.C: Manufacturing products for the organisation
options.D: Delivering goods to customers on behalf of the organisation

questions.explanation: The office manager plans, organises, directs, and controls all office activities — supervising staff, ensuring efficient systems, managing resources, and liaising with other departments.

questions.question: A personal assistant (PA) to an executive primarily

options.A: Manages the company's financial accounts
options.B: Handles correspondence, manages the executive's diary, and coordinates administrative tasks on their behalf
options.C: Designs the company's products
options.D: Supervises all junior staff in the organisation

questions.explanation: A personal assistant supports a senior executive by managing correspondence, diary, meetings, travel arrangements, filing, and general administrative support.

questions.question: A receptionist's primary duty is to

options.A: Keep accounts and prepare financial reports
options.B: Receive visitors, answer telephone calls, and provide first-line information to callers and visitors
options.C: Manage the organisation's social media accounts
options.D: Type and photocopy documents for all departments

questions.explanation: A receptionist is the organisation's first point of contact — welcoming visitors, answering the switchboard, directing callers and visitors, and maintaining the reception area.

questions.question: The duties of a filing clerk include

options.A: Preparing financial statements for management
options.B: Classifying, sorting, and storing documents so they can be retrieved quickly
options.C: Interviewing candidates for vacant positions
options.D: Purchasing office supplies and equipment

questions.explanation: A filing clerk's duties include sorting, classifying, indexing, storing, and retrieving documents in an organised filing system.

questions.question: A memorandum (memo) is a written communication that is

options.A: Sent to external organisations such as customers and suppliers
options.B: Used for internal communication between staff or departments within the same organisation
options.C: A formal legal document signed by the managing director
options.D: A monthly report submitted to government agencies

questions.explanation: A memo is an internal document used to communicate information, instructions, or decisions between staff or departments within the same organisation.

questions.question: A business letter is different from a memo in that a business letter

options.A: Is used only for communication between senior managers
options.B: Is used for external communication with individuals or organisations outside the company
options.C: Does not require a date or reference number
options.D: Is always handwritten rather than typed

questions.explanation: A business letter is used for external communication with customers, suppliers, banks, and government agencies. A memo is used internally.

questions.question: A circular letter is a letter that

options.A: Is written in a circular shape for emphasis
options.B: Is sent to many people simultaneously with the same message
options.C: Concerns only round-trip travel arrangements
options.D: Is used to announce the death of an employee

questions.explanation: A circular letter is a standardised letter sent to many recipients simultaneously — e.g., to inform customers of a price change or new product.

questions.question: An agenda is a document that

options.A: Records the decisions made at a meeting
options.B: Lists the items of business to be discussed at a forthcoming meeting, in the order they will be addressed
options.C: Shows the names of all employees in the organisation
options.D: Summarises the financial performance of the company

questions.explanation: An agenda is sent before a meeting listing items to be discussed in sequence, enabling attendees to prepare and ensuring the meeting runs in an organised manner.

questions.question: Minutes of a meeting are

options.A: The time allocated to each agenda item
options.B: An official written record of the proceedings, discussions, decisions, and actions of a meeting
options.C: A list of all people invited to a meeting
options.D: A summary of the company's performance

questions.explanation: Minutes are the official record of what was discussed and decided at a meeting. They are approved at the next meeting and serve as evidence of decisions and action points.

questions.question: Verbal (oral) communication in the office includes

options.A: Emails, letters, and reports
options.B: Face-to-face conversations, telephone calls, meetings, and presentations
options.C: Notice boards, memos, and circulars
options.D: Filing systems and record-keeping

questions.explanation: Verbal (oral) communication involves spoken exchange — face-to-face, telephone, video calls, meetings, and presentations. It allows immediate feedback.

questions.question: Downward communication in an organisation flows

options.A: From junior staff to senior management
options.B: From management to subordinates — e.g., instructions, policies, and feedback
options.C: Horizontally between staff of equal rank
options.D: From the organisation to external stakeholders

questions.explanation: Downward communication flows from management to subordinates — delivering instructions, policies, feedback, and organisational goals.

questions.question: Upward communication in an organisation flows

options.A: From senior management to junior staff
options.B: From subordinates to management — e.g., reports, suggestions, complaints, and feedback
options.C: Between departments at the same level
options.D: From the company to its customers

questions.explanation: Upward communication flows from staff to management — providing reports, progress updates, suggestions, and feedback essential for informed decision-making.

questions.question: The grapevine in an organisation refers to

options.A: The official communication channels approved by management
options.B: The informal, unofficial communication network through which rumours and gossip spread among staff
options.C: The external communication system used by the company
options.D: The electronic communication system linking all departments

questions.explanation: The grapevine is the informal communication channel — the unofficial network through which rumours and gossip spread among employees, bypassing formal channels.

questions.question: Lateral (horizontal) communication in an organisation flows

options.A: From management to subordinates
options.B: Between people at the same level in the organisation — e.g., between managers of different departments
options.C: From junior staff upward to senior management
options.D: From the organisation to its external stakeholders

questions.explanation: Lateral communication occurs between people at the same organisational level — fostering teamwork and coordination across departments.

questions.question: Barriers to effective communication in an office include

options.A: Clear, concise language and good listening skills
options.B: Language differences, noise, poor listening, information overload, and emotional barriers
options.C: Using too many communication channels simultaneously
options.D: Regular feedback and two-way communication

questions.explanation: Communication barriers prevent messages being received as intended — including noise, language barriers, poor listening, information overload, and cultural differences.

questions.question: An effective communication process requires

options.A: A sender only — no receiver is necessary if the message is clear
options.B: A sender, a message, a channel, a receiver, and feedback
options.C: Only written documentation to avoid misunderstanding
options.D: Hierarchical approval before any message is sent

questions.explanation: Effective communication requires: a sender, a message, a channel, a receiver, and feedback to confirm the message was understood as intended.

questions.question: A report in business communication is a document that

options.A: Only records financial transactions
options.B: Presents investigated or collected information in an organised way to help management make decisions
options.C: Lists all employees and their job descriptions
options.D: Records minutes of a board of directors meeting

questions.explanation: A business report presents factual, organised information to assist management in decision-making, with a defined structure: title, terms of reference, findings, conclusions, and recommendations.

questions.question: Non-verbal communication includes

options.A: Written letters and memos
options.B: Body language, facial expressions, gestures, eye contact, and tone of voice
options.C: Email and text messaging
options.D: Telephone conversations and meetings

questions.explanation: Non-verbal communication conveys messages through body language, facial expressions, gestures, posture, and paralanguage — often reinforcing or contradicting verbal messages.

questions.question: Incoming mail in an office should be handled by

options.A: Only the managing director
options.B: Sorting, opening where appropriate, date-stamping, recording, and distributing to the relevant persons
options.C: Immediately discarding all unsolicited mail
options.D: Filing all mail unopened until the end of the day

questions.explanation: Incoming mail is sorted, opened (confidential mail is not), date-stamped, checked for enclosures, recorded in a mail register, and distributed to relevant persons or departments.

questions.question: A franking machine in the office is used to

options.A: Copy and duplicate documents
options.B: Imprint postage marks on envelopes and parcels, replacing postage stamps
options.C: Record the number of letters sent and received daily
options.D: Seal envelopes automatically

questions.explanation: A franking machine prints an official postage mark directly on envelopes, replacing postage stamps. It speeds up mail processing and allows accurate postage cost recording.

questions.question: Registered mail is a postal service used when

options.A: Sending bulk advertising material to many recipients
options.B: Sending valuable or important documents that require a record of posting and signature on delivery
options.C: Sending standard letters within the same city
options.D: Sending free samples to potential customers

questions.explanation: Registered mail provides a record of posting and requires a signature on delivery — confirming the mail reached its destination. Used for valuable or legally important documents.

questions.question: A courier service differs from standard postal services in that

options.A: Couriers only deliver to government agencies
options.B: Couriers offer faster, tracked, and more secure delivery — often door-to-door with a guaranteed delivery time
options.C: Couriers only handle documents, not packages
options.D: Courier services are always cheaper than postal services

questions.explanation: Courier services provide faster, tracked delivery with guaranteed time windows, greater security, and accountability compared to standard postal services.

questions.question: Filing in an office refers to

options.A: The process of interviewing and selecting new employees
options.B: The systematic arrangement and storage of documents so they can be located and retrieved quickly
options.C: The printing and binding of company reports
options.D: The reconciliation of bank statements with company records

questions.explanation: Filing is the systematic classification, arrangement, and storage of documents in an organised way so they can be found quickly when needed.

questions.question: Alphabetical filing arranges documents

options.A: According to the date they were created
options.B: According to the letters of the alphabet — by name, subject, or location
options.C: In numerical order based on a code assigned to each document
options.D: According to the size and colour of the document

questions.explanation: Alphabetical filing arranges records A-Z by name, subject, or geographical area. It is simple and requires no index.

questions.question: Numerical filing arranges documents

options.A: In alphabetical order by name
options.B: In sequential number order — each file is assigned a unique number
options.C: By the date the document was received
options.D: By the subject matter of the document

questions.explanation: Numerical filing assigns a unique number to each file and arranges them in number order. An alphabetical index is needed to locate files — useful for large volumes of records.

questions.question: Chronological filing arranges documents

options.A: In alphabetical order
options.B: By date — the most recent documents in front or on top
options.C: By the importance of the document
options.D: By the name of the person who created the document

questions.explanation: Chronological filing arranges documents by date, with the most recent in front. Used within other filing systems to order documents within a folder.

questions.question: Subject filing arranges documents

options.A: By the date they were received or sent
options.B: By the topic or subject matter of the document
options.C: By the name of the person who wrote the document
options.D: In numerical order using a reference code

questions.explanation: Subject filing classifies documents by the topic they relate to (e.g., 'Accounts', 'Personnel', 'Marketing'). Useful when documents do not relate to specific individuals.

questions.question: Geographical filing arranges documents

options.A: By alphabetical order of client names
options.B: By location — country, region, city, or territory
options.C: By the size and volume of business transactions
options.D: By the date of the last transaction with the client

questions.explanation: Geographical filing organises records by location. Particularly useful for organisations dealing with clients across different regions or countries.

questions.question: A centralised filing system means

options.A: Files are kept in each individual department
options.B: All records for the entire organisation are kept in one central location managed by a filing department
options.C: Each employee keeps their own personal files
options.D: Files are stored electronically in a central computer system only

questions.explanation: A centralised filing system stores all company records in one location, ensuring uniformity, reducing duplication, and improving security.

questions.question: A decentralised filing system means

options.A: All records are stored in one central location
options.B: Each department keeps its own files relating to its own work
options.C: Only senior staff can access the filing system
options.D: Files are stored alphabetically in each office

questions.explanation: In a decentralised system, each department maintains its own files. This gives departments quick access but may lead to duplication and inconsistency.

questions.question: Cross-referencing in filing is done to

options.A: Check that all documents have been filed in the correct order
options.B: Ensure a document that could be filed under more than one heading can be found regardless of which heading is used
options.C: Keep duplicate copies of all important documents
options.D: Record the number of documents filed each day

questions.explanation: Cross-referencing places a guide card in one location directing the filer to where the document is stored when it could logically be filed under more than one heading.

questions.question: A follow-up (tickler) file is used to

options.A: Record all documents that have been destroyed
options.B: Remind staff of actions or responses due on specific future dates
options.C: Store all documents that cannot be classified
options.D: Keep copies of all outgoing correspondence

questions.explanation: A follow-up (tickler) file is a reminder system — documents needing action on a future date are placed in the relevant date section for retrieval on the correct day.

questions.question: Electronic filing (digital records management) offers which advantage over paper filing?

options.A: Documents cannot be altered or deleted
options.B: Documents can be stored, retrieved, shared, and searched quickly with large volumes stored in minimal physical space
options.C: Electronic files do not require backup
options.D: No training is required to use electronic filing systems

questions.explanation: Electronic filing offers fast retrieval, large storage capacity in small space, easy sharing, simultaneous multi-user access, powerful search functions, and reduced physical storage costs.

questions.question: The 'out card' (absent card) system in filing is used to

options.A: Record documents that have been permanently destroyed
options.B: Show which file has been removed from the filing system, by whom, and when — enabling tracking of files in use
options.C: Mark files that need urgent attention
options.D: List all files that require updating with new information

questions.explanation: When a file is removed from the cabinet, an out card is placed in its position recording who took it, when, and where — allowing others to locate the file and ensuring it is returned.

questions.question: Confidential files in an office should be stored in

options.A: Open shelves accessible to all staff
options.B: Lockable filing cabinets or secure rooms with access restricted to authorised personnel only
options.C: The managing director's personal filing cabinet only
options.D: A digital database accessible to all staff

questions.explanation: Confidential records must be stored securely — in lockable cabinets or secure rooms — with access limited to only those authorised to view them.

questions.question: Alpha-numeric filing combines

options.A: Alphabetical and geographic filing systems
options.B: Alphabetical and numerical filing — grouping records alphabetically by broad categories and numbering files within each category
options.C: Alphabetical filing with colour coding
options.D: Numerical filing with a date-based sub-classification

questions.explanation: Alpha-numeric filing uses letter-based main headings with numbered sub-files within each group, combining the flexibility of alphabetical grouping with the expansibility of numerical coding.

questions.question: A lever arch file is most suitable for

options.A: Storing loose papers temporarily before proper filing
options.B: Holding large volumes of flat documents using a lever mechanism — used for bulk storage of invoices and reports
options.C: Storing cards in alphabetical order using a flip-top mechanism
options.D: Holding microfilm and microfiche for archival storage

questions.explanation: A lever arch file holds large volumes of hole-punched documents. The lever releases the rings for easy addition or removal of documents.

questions.question: A suspension (hanging) file system stores documents in

options.A: Upright folders placed side by side in a drawer
options.B: Folders that hang on rails inside a filing cabinet drawer — allowing easy access and clear labelling
options.C: Horizontal trays stacked on top of each other
options.D: A revolving carousel for rapid access

questions.explanation: Suspension (hanging) files hang from rails in a filing cabinet drawer, clearly labelled and easy to flip through, making retrieval quick.

questions.question: A photocopier is an office machine used to

options.A: Send documents electronically over telephone lines
options.B: Make exact paper copies of documents
options.C: Type letters and reports at high speed
options.D: Scan documents and store them electronically

questions.explanation: A photocopier makes paper duplicates of documents using light, heat, and toner. Modern photocopiers can also scan, print, and fax.

questions.question: A shredder in the office is used to

options.A: Bind documents and reports together
options.B: Destroy confidential documents by cutting them into small pieces
options.C: Sort and separate documents by size
options.D: Create holes in paper for filing in a ring binder

questions.explanation: A document shredder destroys confidential or sensitive documents, protecting against unauthorised access to discarded information.

questions.question: A fax machine (facsimile) is used to

options.A: Make photocopies of documents within the office
options.B: Transmit an exact copy of a document over telephone lines to a remote location
options.C: Print documents directly from a computer
options.D: Bind pages of a report together

questions.explanation: A fax machine scans a document and transmits it as an electronic signal over telephone lines to a receiving fax machine, which prints an exact copy.

questions.question: A scanner in an office is used to

options.A: Check that all staff have arrived for work on time
options.B: Convert physical paper documents into digital images that can be stored or edited on a computer
options.C: Transmit typed messages over telephone lines
options.D: Detect unauthorised entry into the office building

questions.explanation: A scanner converts paper documents and photographs into digital files (JPEG, PDF) that can be stored, edited, emailed, or printed from a computer.

questions.question: A binding machine in the office is used to

options.A: Laminate important documents for protection
options.B: Fasten together the pages of reports or booklets using spiral, comb, or thermal binding
options.C: Punch holes in paper for filing in a ring binder
options.D: Seal envelopes and packages for mailing

questions.explanation: A binding machine fastens document pages together in a professional manner using spiral, comb, thermal, or tape binding methods.

questions.question: A laminator in the office is used to

options.A: Attach paper documents together with staples
options.B: Encase documents in a thin, clear plastic coating to protect them from wear, moisture, and damage
options.C: Create perforations in paper for easy separation
options.D: Scan and print identification cards

questions.explanation: A laminator seals documents between layers of clear plastic film, protecting them from moisture, tearing, and general wear.

questions.question: A PABX (Private Automatic Branch Exchange) is

options.A: A type of computer used for word processing
options.B: A telephone switching system that manages internal and external calls for an organisation
options.C: An accounting software package
options.D: A system for monitoring employee attendance

questions.explanation: A PABX is a private telephone exchange within an organisation. It allows many internal extensions to share external telephone lines and enables free internal calls.

questions.question: A multifunction printer (MFP) in an office can

options.A: Only print documents from a connected computer
options.B: Print, copy, scan, and fax documents from one single machine
options.C: Translate documents from one language to another
options.D: Connect multiple computers wirelessly to the internet

questions.explanation: A multifunction printer combines printer, photocopier, scanner, and fax functions in one device, saving space and cost.

questions.question: A UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) in an office is used to

options.A: Regulate the internet connection speed
options.B: Provide emergency battery backup power to computers if the main electricity supply fails
options.C: Connect multiple computers to a single printer
options.D: Monitor and control electricity usage in the building

questions.explanation: A UPS provides short-term battery backup power when mains electricity fails — giving time to save work and shut down computers properly without data loss.

questions.question: A projector (data projector) in an office is used to

options.A: Print documents from a computer onto paper
options.B: Display computer presentations, videos, or images on a large screen for an audience
options.C: Project light onto office walls for decorative purposes
options.D: Store and retrieve digital files from a remote server

questions.explanation: A data projector connects to a computer and projects the screen image onto a large surface — used for presentations in meetings and training sessions.

questions.question: A time-recording machine (clocking-in machine) is used to

options.A: Schedule meetings and appointments for management
options.B: Record the time employees arrive at and depart from work — for payroll, attendance, and disciplinary purposes
options.C: Measure how long it takes to complete specific office tasks
options.D: Set timers for regular office equipment maintenance

questions.explanation: A time-recording machine records the exact time employees arrive and leave — used for attendance monitoring, overtime calculation, and payroll processing.

questions.question: A paper guillotine (trimmer) in an office is used to

options.A: Destroy confidential documents by cutting them into strips
options.B: Cut paper or card to a precise size with a clean, straight edge
options.C: Create perforations in paper for filing
options.D: Bind documents together using heat

questions.explanation: A guillotine provides a straight, clean cut when trimming paper, card, or photographs to an exact size.

questions.question: Office stationery refers to

options.A: All the furniture used in the office
options.B: Writing materials, paper, envelopes, pens, and other consumable supplies used in the office
options.C: All electronic equipment used in the office
options.D: The cleaning materials used to maintain the office

questions.explanation: Office stationery includes all writing and printing materials — paper, envelopes, letterheads, pens, pencils, clips, staples, and folders — used up in the course of office work.

questions.question: The purpose of a stock card (bin card) for office supplies is to

options.A: Show the names of staff authorised to collect office supplies
options.B: Record the quantity of each item in stock, goods received, goods issued, and the balance remaining
options.C: List all suppliers from whom stationery is purchased
options.D: Show the prices of all office supplies purchased in the year

questions.explanation: A stock card records receipts, issues, and the running balance of each item, enabling stock levels to be monitored and reorder points identified.

questions.question: Letterheaded (headed) paper is used for

options.A: All internal memos and notices
options.B: Official external correspondence — displaying the company's name, logo, address, and contact details pre-printed at the top
options.C: Filing and storing internal company records
options.D: Recording petty cash expenditures

questions.explanation: Letterheaded paper has the company's name, logo, and contact details pre-printed. It is used for official external letters, giving a professional, branded image.

questions.question: When receiving a visitor in an office, the receptionist should first

options.A: Ask the visitor to wait outside until the relevant officer is free
options.B: Greet the visitor courteously, find out their name and purpose of visit, and notify the relevant person
options.C: Direct the visitor immediately to the relevant department without checking
options.D: Request the visitor to complete a lengthy questionnaire

questions.explanation: The receptionist should greet visitors warmly, get their name and purpose of visit, check whether the person they want to see is available, notify them, and show the visitor to the waiting area.

questions.question: A visitors' book in an office is used to

options.A: Record all telephone calls received by the office
options.B: Record the names, organisations, purpose of visit, and time of arrival and departure of all visitors
options.C: List all employees and their job titles
options.D: Store business cards collected from visitors

questions.explanation: A visitors' book records all visitor details — name, organisation, purpose, person visited, arrival, and departure — for security, reference, and auditing.

questions.question: A well-organised reception area should

options.A: Be located away from the main entrance so visitors must search for it
options.B: Be easily accessible, clearly signposted, welcoming, and provide comfortable seating and current reading material for visitors
options.C: Restrict access to visitors by requiring appointment-only entry
options.D: Be staffed only during peak visiting hours

questions.explanation: The reception area is the organisation's first impression. It should be welcoming, clearly signposted, comfortably furnished, and staffed by a professional receptionist.

questions.question: When answering a business telephone call, the correct procedure is to

options.A: Answer as quickly as possible and ask the caller to hold
options.B: Identify the organisation and yourself, greet the caller, and offer assistance promptly
options.C: Wait for the caller to introduce themselves before speaking
options.D: Ask for the caller's name and callback number and then put the phone down

questions.explanation: A business call should be answered promptly by identifying the company and the person answering — e.g., 'Good morning, ABC Ltd, John speaking, how may I help you?'

questions.question: A telephone message form is used to

options.A: Record the cost of all business telephone calls
options.B: Record the details of a telephone message when the intended recipient is unavailable
options.C: Show the telephone directory for the organisation
options.D: Log all calls made by each employee for billing purposes

questions.explanation: A telephone message form records: caller's name, organisation, telephone number, time of call, message content, and action required — for accurate relay to the recipient.

questions.question: Which of the following is good telephone etiquette in an office?

options.A: Eating or chewing food while on a call
options.B: Speaking clearly, listening actively, not interrupting, and ensuring the caller's query is resolved
options.C: Putting callers on hold for long periods without explanation
options.D: Using technical jargon to impress the caller

questions.explanation: Good telephone etiquette includes answering promptly, speaking clearly, listening carefully, not interrupting, being polite and helpful, and taking accurate messages.

questions.question: The imprest system of petty cash management means

options.A: The petty cashier receives a different amount each month
options.B: A fixed float is given to the petty cashier; at each reimbursement period, they are topped up by the exact amount spent to restore the float
options.C: The petty cashier can spend any amount without approval
options.D: Petty cash is only used for purchases above a certain amount

questions.explanation: Under the imprest system, the petty cashier holds a fixed float. At the end of each period, the main cashier reimburses exactly what was spent, restoring the float.

questions.question: Petty cash is used to pay for

options.A: Large purchases such as computers and furniture
options.B: Small, routine cash expenses such as postage, stationery, bus fares, and refreshments
options.C: Monthly salaries of all office staff
options.D: Payments to major suppliers by bank transfer

questions.explanation: Petty cash covers small, everyday expenditures inconvenient to pay by cheque — e.g., postage stamps, taxi fares, stationery items.

questions.question: A petty cash voucher is used to

options.A: Record the total petty cash fund at the start of the month
options.B: Authorise and record each individual petty cash payment
options.C: Request a reimbursement of the petty cash fund from the bank
options.D: Record cash sales made by the business

questions.explanation: A petty cash voucher records the date, amount, reason for payment, and signatures of the person requesting and the person authorising each petty cash payment.

questions.question: The Health and Safety at Work Act requires employers to

options.A: Provide the highest salary possible to all employees
options.B: Ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety, and welfare of all employees and visitors
options.C: Allow employees to work from home whenever they choose
options.D: Provide free meals to all employees during working hours

questions.explanation: Health and safety legislation requires employers to provide a safe working environment, safe equipment, adequate training, and safe systems of work.

questions.question: Ergonomics in an office setting is the study of

options.A: The electronic systems used in the office
options.B: How to design the workplace, equipment, and tasks to fit the worker — reducing discomfort, injury, and fatigue
options.C: The economic efficiency of the office
options.D: The number of staff required to run the office efficiently

questions.explanation: Ergonomics designs workplaces and equipment to fit human physical and cognitive needs — reducing the risk of repetitive strain injury, back pain, and eye strain.

questions.question: Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI) in an office is most commonly caused by

options.A: Lifting heavy packages in the mail room
options.B: Prolonged repetitive tasks such as excessive keyboard use and mouse clicking
options.C: Excessive standing during presentations
options.D: Exposure to cold air from office air conditioning

questions.explanation: RSI affects muscles, tendons, and nerves of the arms and wrists, caused by repetitive or awkward movements — commonly from excessive keyboard or mouse use.

questions.question: In the event of a fire in the office, the first action to take is to

options.A: Collect personal belongings before evacuating
options.B: Raise the alarm, evacuate the building using the designated routes, and call the fire service
options.C: Attempt to fight the fire with whatever is available
options.D: Call the managing director before doing anything else

questions.explanation: In a fire emergency: raise the alarm, evacuate via designated routes to the assembly point, and call the fire service. Fight only very small fires if trained to do so.

questions.question: A risk assessment in the workplace involves

options.A: Assessing the financial risk of a business investment
options.B: Identifying hazards in the workplace, evaluating the likelihood and severity of harm, and implementing controls to reduce risk
options.C: Testing new employees for health problems before starting work
options.D: Calculating the insurance premium for employer's liability insurance

questions.explanation: A risk assessment identifies hazards, assesses who might be harmed and how seriously, and determines controls needed to eliminate or reduce risk.

questions.question: A fire extinguisher with a red label and water content should be used on

options.A: Electrical fires
options.B: Class A fires involving solid materials such as paper, wood, and textiles
options.C: Flammable liquid fires
options.D: Cooking oil fires

questions.explanation: Water (red label) extinguishers are suitable for Class A fires (solid combustibles). They must NEVER be used on electrical fires (risk of electrocution) or flammable liquid fires.

questions.question: Manual handling training in an office teaches employees

options.A: How to handle customer complaints professionally
options.B: Correct techniques for lifting, carrying, and moving loads to avoid back injuries
options.C: How to operate heavy manufacturing machinery
options.D: How to safely use office software and computers

questions.explanation: Manual handling training teaches safe lifting techniques — bending the knees, keeping the load close to the body, not twisting the spine — to prevent musculoskeletal injuries.

questions.question: Recruitment in an organisation refers to

options.A: Training employees to perform their current jobs better
options.B: The process of attracting and identifying qualified candidates for a job vacancy
options.C: Dismissing underperforming employees
options.D: Assessing employees for promotion

questions.explanation: Recruitment is the process of attracting qualified candidates to fill a vacancy — through advertising, job boards, agencies, or internal promotions.

questions.question: A job description is a document that

options.A: Lists the educational qualifications required for a job
options.B: Details the title, duties, responsibilities, and working conditions of a specific job
options.C: Records the salary and benefits offered to a new employee
options.D: Shows the organisational hierarchy of the company

questions.explanation: A job description outlines what a job involves — job title, reporting structure, duties, responsibilities, working conditions, and key performance indicators.

questions.question: A person specification outlines

options.A: The daily tasks to be performed in a specific job
options.B: The qualities, skills, qualifications, and experience required of the person who will fill the job
options.C: The organisational chart showing reporting relationships
options.D: The salary, benefits, and working hours for the position

questions.explanation: A person specification describes the ideal candidate — qualifications, experience, skills, and personal attributes (essential and desirable) — used to assess candidates during selection.

questions.question: Induction training is given to

options.A: All senior managers before attending an international conference
options.B: New employees to help them become familiar with the organisation, its policies, colleagues, and their role
options.C: Employees who have been promoted to a new position
options.D: Employees who have been underperforming in their current role

questions.explanation: Induction (orientation) training introduces new employees to the organisation — its history, culture, policies, facilities, health and safety procedures, and their specific role.

questions.question: Performance appraisal is a process used to

options.A: Recruit new staff to fill vacant positions
options.B: Systematically evaluate an employee's work performance against agreed objectives and standards
options.C: Calculate the wages and salaries of all employees
options.D: Decide which employees should be made redundant

questions.explanation: Performance appraisal formally assesses how well employees are performing — identifying strengths, areas for improvement, training needs, and informing promotion and pay decisions.

questions.question: Redundancy in employment means

options.A: An employee who is underperforming is dismissed for misconduct
options.B: A situation where an employee's job no longer exists due to business restructuring, closure, or reduced work
options.C: An employee who repeatedly arrives late to work
options.D: A temporary suspension of an employee pending investigation

questions.explanation: Redundancy occurs when a job role is no longer needed — due to restructuring, downsizing, or automation — not because of the employee's performance. The employee is entitled to redundancy pay.

questions.question: An employment contract is a document that

options.A: Shows the organisation chart of the company
options.B: Sets out the terms and conditions of employment agreed between the employer and the employee
options.C: Records the employee's performance appraisal results
options.D: Lists the names of all staff employed by the organisation

questions.explanation: An employment contract is a legally binding agreement specifying: job title, duties, pay, hours, holiday entitlement, notice period, and other terms and conditions.

questions.question: Disciplinary procedures in an office are used to

options.A: Reward high-performing employees
options.B: Address employee misconduct or poor performance in a fair, consistent, and legally compliant manner
options.C: Promote employees who have served for a specified period
options.D: Calculate the wages and salaries of all employees

questions.explanation: Disciplinary procedures provide a fair process for dealing with misconduct — typically: verbal warning, written warning, final written warning, dismissal — ensuring consistent and legally defensible treatment.

questions.question: A curriculum vitae (CV) is a document that

options.A: Lists all the terms and conditions of an employment contract
options.B: Summarises an applicant's personal details, qualifications, work experience, skills, and achievements — submitted when applying for a job
options.C: Records the results of a performance appraisal
options.D: Shows the organisational hierarchy of a company

questions.explanation: A CV is a personal marketing document submitted with a job application, summarising education, qualifications, work experience, and skills to help employers shortlist candidates.

questions.question: An interview in recruitment is used to

options.A: Test an applicant's typing speed and accuracy
options.B: Assess a candidate's suitability for a job through face-to-face questioning
options.C: Check the applicant's financial credit history
options.D: Provide the successful applicant with their employment contract

questions.explanation: A selection interview allows employers to assess candidates — evaluating personality, communication skills, knowledge, and fit with the role and organisation.

questions.question: A staff training and development programme aims to

options.A: Select candidates for promotion only
options.B: Improve the knowledge, skills, and competencies of employees to enhance performance and career development
options.C: Evaluate the performance of all employees at year end
options.D: Reduce staff numbers to cut costs

questions.explanation: Staff training and development improves employees' job-specific skills and broader competencies, increasing productivity, quality, job satisfaction, and readiness for greater responsibility.

questions.question: Job rotation as a staff development technique involves

options.A: Replacing underperforming staff with new recruits
options.B: Moving employees between different jobs or tasks at regular intervals to broaden their skills and experience
options.C: Rotating staff shifts to cover different working hours
options.D: Assigning employees to new positions as a disciplinary measure

questions.explanation: Job rotation moves employees through different roles systematically — broadening skills, reducing boredom, and making them more flexible and versatile.

questions.question: Job enrichment as a motivational technique involves

options.A: Giving employees more tasks of the same level of complexity
options.B: Giving employees more responsibility, autonomy, and challenge in their existing role to increase motivation and satisfaction
options.C: Increasing employees' salaries to motivate them
options.D: Providing employees with free meals and transport as additional benefits

questions.explanation: Job enrichment (Herzberg) adds vertical job content — more responsibility, decision-making authority, and challenging tasks — addressing motivating factors rather than just hygiene factors.

questions.question: Records management in an office refers to

options.A: The management of the company's financial accounts
options.B: The systematic control of the creation, use, maintenance, storage, retrieval, and disposal of records
options.C: The management of the company's physical assets
options.D: The process of recruiting and managing office staff

questions.explanation: Records management covers the entire lifecycle of records — from creation through active use to storage (retention) and ultimate disposal or permanent archiving.

questions.question: A retention schedule in records management specifies

options.A: The time taken to retrieve a specific record from the filing system
options.B: How long different types of records should be kept before they are reviewed for disposal
options.C: The number of copies to be made of each important document
options.D: The order in which documents should be filed

questions.explanation: A retention schedule specifies how long each type of record must be kept (for legal, regulatory, or operational reasons) before being reviewed, archived, or destroyed.

questions.question: The purpose of document archiving is to

options.A: Destroy documents that are no longer needed
options.B: Transfer inactive but potentially valuable records to long-term storage for preservation and future reference
options.C: Create digital copies of all paper documents
options.D: Shred all documents older than five years

questions.explanation: Archiving transfers inactive records to long-term storage — freeing office space while preserving records that may be needed in the future.

questions.question: Disposal of records refers to

options.A: Selling old office furniture and equipment
options.B: The final action taken on records — either permanent destruction (shredding) or permanent preservation (archiving)
options.C: Moving records from the filing room to the accounts department
options.D: Photocopying records before they are filed away

questions.explanation: Records disposal is the final step in the records management cycle — records are either destroyed when no longer needed or transferred to permanent archives.

questions.question: Active records are

options.A: Records that have been archived for permanent preservation
options.B: Records that are in current, regular use and need to be immediately accessible
options.C: Records that have been identified for destruction
options.D: Records stored on microfilm for compact storage

questions.explanation: Active records are used frequently in day-to-day operations and must be readily accessible, typically stored in the office filing system.

questions.question: The Data Protection Act requires that personal data stored by an organisation must be

options.A: Shared with all employees who request access
options.B: Kept accurate, up to date, secure, and used only for its stated purpose — with access limited to authorised personnel
options.C: Destroyed after six months regardless of its nature
options.D: Stored only in physical paper files for security

questions.explanation: Data protection legislation requires organisations to collect, store, and process personal data lawfully, securely, and transparently — keeping it accurate, limiting retention, and restricting access.

questions.question: The duties of a secretary include

options.A: Manufacturing products and managing production staff
options.B: Managing correspondence, maintaining diaries, organising meetings, filing, and providing administrative support to management
options.C: Designing advertising campaigns for the company
options.D: Preparing financial statements and auditing company accounts

questions.explanation: A secretary handles correspondence, maintains the executive's diary, organises meetings (agendas, minutes), manages filing, answers telephones, and provides general administrative support.

questions.question: Shorthand writing is a skill used by secretaries to

options.A: Write letters in a secret code that only the secretary can read
options.B: Record spoken dictation quickly using a system of abbreviated symbols and strokes
options.C: Type documents at high speed on a computer keyboard
options.D: Summarise financial reports into short paragraphs

questions.explanation: Shorthand is a rapid writing system using symbols or abbreviations to record spoken words quickly — traditionally used by secretaries to take dictation from executives.

questions.question: A secretary preparing minutes of a meeting should include

options.A: Their own personal opinions on the discussions
options.B: The date, time, venue, attendees, apologies, matters arising, agenda items discussed, decisions made, and action points
options.C: Only the decisions reached without any discussion details
options.D: A full transcript of every word spoken during the meeting

questions.explanation: Minutes should record: date, time, location, attendees, apologies, matters arising, key discussion points, decisions made, actions assigned, and the time the meeting closed.

questions.question: A quorum at a meeting refers to

options.A: The chairperson's opening speech
options.B: The minimum number of members that must be present for the meeting to be valid and legally conduct business
options.C: The total number of items on the agenda
options.D: The maximum time allowed for any single agenda item

questions.explanation: A quorum is the minimum number of qualified members who must be present for a meeting to be validly constituted and for its decisions to be legally binding.

questions.question: The chairperson of a meeting is responsible for

options.A: Taking notes and writing the minutes of the meeting
options.B: Controlling the meeting, ensuring agenda items are covered, maintaining order, and facilitating decisions
options.C: Preparing the agenda and distributing it to participants
options.D: Counting votes and announcing the results of elections

questions.explanation: The chairperson opens and closes the meeting, controls proceedings, ensures all agenda items are discussed, maintains order, and declares results of votes.

questions.question: An Annual General Meeting (AGM) is a meeting that

options.A: Takes place every week to review operational performance
options.B: A company is legally required to hold at least once a year to present annual accounts, elect directors, and allow shareholders to vote on resolutions
options.C: Is called to deal with urgent matters requiring immediate attention
options.D: Is held only when the company is in financial difficulty

questions.explanation: An AGM is a statutory annual meeting where annual reports and accounts are presented, directors elected, dividends declared, and shareholders exercise their rights.

questions.question: An Extraordinary General Meeting (EGM) is called to

options.A: Conduct routine business that cannot wait until the next AGM
options.B: Discuss urgent or exceptional matters that arise between AGMs — such as a proposed merger or change to the company's constitution
options.C: Review the performance of individual directors
options.D: Discuss the company's annual budget for the coming year

questions.explanation: An EGM is called when urgent or important issues arise between AGMs — such as a proposed takeover, major capital expenditure, or amendment to the company's articles.

questions.question: A motion at a meeting is

options.A: The physical movement of the chairperson during a meeting
options.B: A formal proposal put before a meeting for discussion and a decision
options.C: A request by a member to adjourn the meeting
options.D: The process of transferring the meeting to a new venue

questions.explanation: A motion is a formal proposal put forward by a member for consideration and a vote. If passed, it becomes a resolution — an official decision.

questions.question: A resolution at a meeting is

options.A: The chairperson's summary of the discussion
options.B: A motion that has been formally voted upon and passed by the required majority — becoming an official decision
options.C: An action point assigned to a specific committee member
options.D: A written record of the entire meeting proceedings

questions.explanation: A resolution is a motion that has been put to a vote and passed — becoming an official decision of the organisation, recorded formally in the minutes.

questions.question: Matters arising in meeting minutes refers to

options.A: New items of business to be discussed for the first time
options.B: A review of action points from the previous meeting to check what has been done and what remains outstanding
options.C: The chairperson's introductory remarks
options.D: Issues raised by members after the formal agenda has been completed

questions.explanation: 'Matters arising' reviews what was decided at the previous meeting — checking whether action points have been completed and updating on outstanding matters.

questions.question: Proxy voting at a meeting allows

options.A: All votes to be counted twice for accuracy
options.B: A member who cannot attend to authorise another person to vote on their behalf
options.C: Electronic voting to replace physical attendance
options.D: The chairperson to override the result of any vote

questions.explanation: A proxy is when a member who cannot attend appoints another person (proxy) to attend and vote on their behalf, authorised by a proxy form.

questions.question: An adjournment of a meeting means

options.A: The meeting has been permanently cancelled
options.B: The meeting is suspended and will be resumed at a later date or time
options.C: A vote has been taken on all items and the meeting is closed
options.D: A new member has been invited to join the meeting

questions.explanation: Adjournment suspends a meeting to be resumed at another time — because business was not completed, more information is needed, or due to a breach of quorum.

questions.question: Office management is concerned with

options.A: Only the typing and filing duties in the office
options.B: Planning, organising, directing, and controlling all office functions to achieve organisational objectives efficiently
options.C: Managing only the financial records of the organisation
options.D: Designing and decorating the office environment

questions.explanation: Office management involves planning, organising, directing, and controlling all administrative and clerical functions to ensure the office supports the organisation's goals.

questions.question: Delegation in office management means

options.A: Refusing to carry out tasks assigned by a superior
options.B: Assigning tasks, authority, and responsibility to subordinates while remaining accountable for the outcome
options.C: Passing all decision-making to external consultants
options.D: Reducing the workload by eliminating unnecessary tasks

questions.explanation: Delegation involves assigning specific tasks and the authority to carry them out to subordinates. The manager remains ultimately accountable but empowers staff to act independently.

questions.question: Span of control in office management refers to

options.A: The total area (in square metres) of the office space
options.B: The number of subordinates a manager directly supervises
options.C: The range of products or services offered by the organisation
options.D: The extent of a manager's financial authority

questions.explanation: Span of control is the number of subordinates reporting directly to one manager. A wide span suits routine work; a narrow span suits complex work requiring close supervision.

questions.question: An organisation chart is a document that

options.A: Shows the financial performance of the organisation for the year
options.B: Visually shows the structure of the organisation — including reporting relationships, departments, and levels of authority
options.C: Lists all the products and services offered by the organisation
options.D: Records the goals and objectives of each department

questions.explanation: An organisation chart is a diagram showing the hierarchy — departments, job positions, and reporting relationships — providing a clear picture of the organisational structure.

questions.question: The chain of command in an organisation refers to

options.A: A series of computer commands used in office software
options.B: The line of authority and responsibility that links every person from top management to the lowest level
options.C: A sequence of events that must occur before a purchase order is approved
options.D: The order in which tasks must be completed in the office

questions.explanation: The chain of command is the formal line of authority through which orders flow from senior management down and accountability flows upward.

questions.question: Authority in an organisation refers to

options.A: The physical strength needed to manage a large team
options.B: The legitimate right to give instructions, make decisions, and require others to carry out tasks
options.C: The technical skill and expertise of a manager
options.D: The number of years a manager has worked for the organisation

questions.explanation: Authority is the formal, legitimate right given to a position in the hierarchy to give instructions, make decisions, and allocate resources.

questions.question: Coordination in office management refers to

options.A: The physical arrangement of office furniture and equipment
options.B: The process of linking and integrating the activities of different departments and individuals to achieve organisational goals
options.C: Managing the organisation's bank accounts
options.D: Counting and controlling all office supplies

questions.explanation: Coordination integrates the activities of different departments and individuals — ensuring they work harmoniously toward common goals without duplication or conflict.

questions.question: Work simplification in office management aims to

options.A: Reduce the number of staff by making work more complex
options.B: Eliminate unnecessary steps in a procedure to make work faster, easier, and more efficient
options.C: Simplify the reporting structure of the organisation
options.D: Reduce the range of services provided by the office

questions.explanation: Work simplification analyses existing procedures to eliminate waste, duplication, and unnecessary steps — making processes faster, cheaper, and less prone to error.

questions.question: Management by Objectives (MBO) is a technique that involves

options.A: Managing the office exclusively by verbal instructions
options.B: Setting specific, measurable goals jointly agreed by managers and subordinates — used to guide, motivate, and appraise performance
options.C: Managing the business purely based on financial objectives
options.D: Allowing managers to set any objectives without consulting staff

questions.explanation: MBO involves managers and subordinates jointly setting specific, measurable objectives aligned to organisational goals, with performance appraised against agreed objectives.

questions.question: Budgetary control in office management involves

options.A: Counting the number of employees who arrive late each month
options.B: Planning and comparing actual expenditure against budgeted amounts to control costs
options.C: Designing the annual budget for the entire organisation
options.D: Approving all purchase orders above a certain value

questions.explanation: Budgetary control involves setting budgets, monitoring actual expenditure, comparing with budgets, and taking corrective action when variances occur.

questions.question: A procedure manual in an office provides

options.A: A record of the financial procedures approved by the board
options.B: Detailed step-by-step instructions for carrying out all standard office procedures consistently
options.C: A list of names and duties of all office staff
options.D: The targets and objectives for each department

questions.explanation: A procedures manual documents how tasks should be performed — ensuring consistency, quality, and compliance, and supporting training of new staff.

questions.question: Word processing software in an office is used for

options.A: Managing the company's financial accounts
options.B: Creating, editing, formatting, and printing text-based documents such as letters, reports, and memos
options.C: Creating spreadsheets with financial calculations
options.D: Managing the company's database of customers

questions.explanation: Word processing software (e.g., Microsoft Word) enables creation, editing, formatting, saving, and printing of text documents — replacing the typewriter with far greater flexibility.

questions.question: Spreadsheet software in an office is used for

options.A: Creating and editing text documents
options.B: Organising numerical data, performing calculations, and creating charts and graphs
options.C: Managing a database of customer information
options.D: Designing promotional materials for marketing

questions.explanation: Spreadsheet software (e.g., Microsoft Excel) organises data in rows and columns, performs calculations, and creates charts — used for budgets, accounts, and financial planning.

questions.question: Database software in an office is used for

options.A: Typing letters and reports
options.B: Storing, organising, and retrieving large volumes of structured data — such as customer records and inventory
options.C: Preparing presentations for meetings
options.D: Sending and receiving emails

questions.explanation: Database software organises and manages large volumes of structured data — enabling efficient storage, retrieval, sorting, and analysis of records such as customer databases.

questions.question: A Local Area Network (LAN) in an office is

options.A: A worldwide network connecting millions of computers globally
options.B: A computer network that connects devices within a limited area such as a single office building
options.C: A network connecting multiple company branches across different cities
options.D: A software application for managing office communications

questions.explanation: A LAN connects computers and peripherals within a limited geographic area (one building or campus), allowing staff to share files, printers, and internet connections.

questions.question: Cloud computing in an office context refers to

options.A: Computing performed during cloudy weather conditions
options.B: Storing and accessing data and applications over the internet rather than on a local computer or server
options.C: Using portable laptop computers instead of desktops
options.D: A network of computers connected through a wireless radio system

questions.explanation: Cloud computing stores data and runs applications on remote servers accessed via the internet — enabling anywhere/anytime access, automatic backups, and cost savings on hardware.

questions.question: An intranet in an organisation is

options.A: The same as the internet but faster
options.B: A private internal network that provides information and services to employees within the organisation only
options.C: A network connecting the organisation to all its suppliers and customers
options.D: An internal email system for top management only

questions.explanation: An intranet is a private internal network accessible only to employees — used to share company documents, policies, news, forms, and internal communications securely.

questions.question: Videoconferencing in an office enables

options.A: Viewing pre-recorded training videos at the employee's convenience
options.B: Real-time audio and visual communication between participants in different locations — replacing the need for physical meetings
options.C: Automatically recording and transcribing all phone conversations
options.D: Broadcasting company announcements via email

questions.explanation: Videoconferencing (Zoom, Teams, Skype) enables real-time face-to-face communication between people in different locations — reducing travel costs while maintaining visual interaction.

questions.question: Teleconferencing in an office allows

options.A: Television broadcasting of company advertisements
options.B: Multiple people in different locations to hold a voice conference call simultaneously without physical travel
options.C: Individual employees to watch television during work breaks
options.D: Management to monitor employee telephone calls for training purposes

questions.explanation: Teleconferencing (audio conferencing) allows multiple parties in different locations to participate in the same call simultaneously — enabling meetings and briefings without travel.

questions.question: A digital signature in electronic documents is used to

options.A: Scan a handwritten signature and add it to a document
options.B: Provide a secure, verifiable electronic means of authenticating the identity of the signer and confirming a document has not been altered
options.C: Format the signature field at the bottom of a digital form
options.D: Print a signature automatically on letters using a stamp

questions.explanation: A digital signature uses cryptographic technology to authenticate the signer's identity and confirm the document's integrity — it cannot be forged and proves the document has not been tampered with.

questions.question: A network printer in an office is

options.A: A printer reserved for printing sensitive financial reports
options.B: A printer connected to the office network so multiple computers and users can share it
options.C: A portable printer that can be moved between desks
options.D: A printer that can only print in black and white

questions.explanation: A network printer is connected to the office LAN, enabling all computers on the network to send print jobs to it — eliminating the need for individual printers at each workstation.

questions.question: The main advantage of word processing over traditional typewriting is that

options.A: Word processing produces documents faster but with more errors
options.B: Documents can be easily edited, saved, stored, and reprinted without retyping the entire document
options.C: Word processing requires no electricity to operate
options.D: Word-processed documents are legally superior to typewritten ones

questions.explanation: Word processing allows easy editing, saving for reuse, formatting options, and reprinting without retyping — offering far greater efficiency and flexibility.

questions.question: Mail merge in word processing is a feature that allows

options.A: Merging two separate email accounts into one
options.B: Producing personalised letters by combining a standard template with a database of individual names and addresses
options.C: Sending an email to multiple recipients simultaneously
options.D: Combining multiple documents into a single PDF file

questions.explanation: Mail merge combines a standard letter template with a data source to automatically produce individually personalised letters — used for circular letters and bulk correspondence.

questions.question: The autocorrect feature in word processing software is designed to

options.A: Automatically format a document according to a pre-set template
options.B: Automatically correct common spelling mistakes and typos as text is typed
options.C: Check that grammar in a document is correct
options.D: Automatically save a document every few minutes

questions.explanation: Autocorrect automatically detects and corrects common misspellings and typos as the user types — speeding up typing and reducing basic errors.

questions.question: Confidentiality in an office context means

options.A: Keeping all financial records locked in the managing director's safe
options.B: Protecting sensitive information from unauthorised access or disclosure — only sharing it with those who need to know
options.C: Refusing to communicate with external organisations
options.D: Destroying all company records after they have been used

questions.explanation: Confidentiality requires that sensitive information (personnel data, financial details, client information) is only accessible to authorised individuals.

questions.question: A password policy in an office is used to

options.A: Prevent employees from accessing the internet during working hours
options.B: Ensure computer systems are protected from unauthorised access by requiring strong, regularly changed passwords
options.C: Record how many times each employee uses the computer each day
options.D: Prevent the use of social media during working hours

questions.explanation: A password policy sets rules for creating and managing passwords — minimum length, complexity, regular changes, and prohibition of sharing — to protect systems from unauthorised access.

questions.question: Access control systems in an office are used to

options.A: Control the number of telephone calls made by each employee
options.B: Restrict entry to premises or specific areas to authorised personnel only — using key cards, PIN codes, or biometrics
options.C: Manage the access times of part-time employees
options.D: Block access to unauthorised websites on office computers

questions.explanation: Access control systems restrict physical access to buildings or rooms to authorised personnel, improving security of people, assets, and information.

questions.question: A line (scalar) organisation structure is one in which

options.A: Authority and responsibility flow horizontally across departments
options.B: Authority flows vertically from top to bottom — each person is responsible to only one superior
options.C: All managers have equal authority regardless of their position
options.D: Decision-making is shared among all staff at every level

questions.explanation: A line (scalar) structure has a clear vertical chain of command from top to bottom. Each employee reports to one boss (unity of command), making authority and responsibility clear.

questions.question: A functional organisation structure groups employees by

options.A: Geographic region or territory
options.B: The type of work they perform — such as finance, marketing, production, and human resources
options.C: The products or services they work on
options.D: Customer segment or market served

questions.explanation: A functional structure organises the business into departments based on function. Each department is managed by a functional specialist, enabling specialisation and efficiency.

questions.question: The unity of command principle in management states that

options.A: All managers must have equal authority in the organisation
options.B: Each employee should receive orders from only one superior to avoid confusion and conflict
options.C: All decisions must be made unanimously by the management team
options.D: Commands must be given in writing and never verbally

questions.explanation: Unity of command means each subordinate reports to only one boss. Multiple bosses can give conflicting instructions, causing confusion and undermining accountability.

questions.question: Centralisation in management refers to

options.A: All activities taking place in the centre of the organisation's geographic area
options.B: The concentration of decision-making authority at the top levels of the organisation
options.C: Locating all office services in one central department
options.D: The equal distribution of authority among all managers

questions.explanation: Centralisation concentrates decision-making authority with top management. This ensures consistency and control but can slow responses.

questions.question: Decentralisation in management refers to

options.A: Moving the company's head office to a different location
options.B: Distributing decision-making authority to lower levels or branch managers within the organisation
options.C: Closing down departments that are not profitable
options.D: Replacing human workers with automated systems

questions.explanation: Decentralisation distributes decision-making to lower management levels — enabling faster local decisions, greater flexibility, and improved staff motivation.

questions.question: A notice is a document used to

options.A: Record the decisions made at a meeting
options.B: Inform members or staff about a forthcoming meeting or important event
options.C: Communicate confidentially between two senior managers
options.D: Record all financial transactions of the organisation

questions.explanation: A notice is used to inform concerned parties about a forthcoming meeting — giving the date, time, venue, and type of meeting — sent well in advance to allow preparation.

questions.question: A progress report in an office is used to

options.A: Record the attendance of employees at training sessions
options.B: Update management on the status of an ongoing project, task, or investigation
options.C: Show the financial progress of the company during the year
options.D: Record any changes in the organisation's personnel

questions.explanation: A progress report keeps management informed of developments on an ongoing project — covering achievements, remaining work, problems encountered, and planned next steps.

questions.question: A staff handbook is a document given to employees to

options.A: Record the minutes of all departmental meetings
options.B: Provide information about company policies, rules, procedures, benefits, and employee rights and responsibilities
options.C: Show each employee their individual performance targets
options.D: List all the products and services offered by the company

questions.explanation: A staff handbook informs employees about company history, culture, policies (absence, disciplinary), pay and benefits, health and safety, and their rights and obligations.

questions.question: Adequate lighting in an office is important because

options.A: It saves electricity costs for the organisation
options.B: Poor lighting causes eye strain, headaches, and reduces productivity and accuracy
options.C: It makes the office look more attractive to visitors
options.D: Bright lighting is required by health and safety law in all offices

questions.explanation: Proper lighting reduces eye strain, headaches, and fatigue, improving staff productivity, accuracy, and well-being.

questions.question: Office ventilation is important because

options.A: It helps maintain the security of the office
options.B: Adequate air circulation reduces stuffiness, heat, and odours, improving staff comfort and productivity
options.C: It is required for the operation of all office equipment
options.D: It reduces the amount of natural light entering the office

questions.explanation: Good ventilation maintains comfortable temperature and air quality — reducing heat, humidity, and CO2 that cause fatigue and drowsiness.

questions.question: Office furniture should be arranged to

options.A: Impress visitors with an attractive layout regardless of practicality
options.B: Facilitate workflow, ensure safety, maximise space, and provide comfort to employees
options.C: Keep all desks facing the same direction regardless of natural light
options.D: Separate senior staff from junior staff at all times

questions.explanation: Good office furniture arrangement should support efficient workflow, comply with health and safety requirements, maximise available space, and provide comfortable ergonomic working positions.

questions.question: Noise control in the office is important because

options.A: Noise is legally prohibited in all commercial premises
options.B: Excessive noise causes distraction, stress, and reduced concentration, lowering productivity and increasing errors
options.C: Noise damages office equipment over time
options.D: Clients and customers expect complete silence in all offices

questions.explanation: Excessive noise is a major source of distraction and stress, reducing concentration, increasing errors, and leading to fatigue. Sound-absorbing materials and acoustic panels help.

questions.question: A standard operating procedure (SOP) in an office is

options.A: A list of all employees and their job descriptions
options.B: A documented step-by-step process that specifies how routine tasks should be carried out consistently
options.C: A financial statement showing operating costs
options.D: A set of government regulations that all offices must follow

questions.explanation: An SOP is a detailed written procedure describing how to carry out a specific routine task — ensuring consistency, quality, and compliance.

questions.question: A purchase order in an office is a document used to

options.A: Record goods received from a supplier
options.B: Formally authorise the purchase of goods or services from a specified supplier
